coredata.dc:title: Pd and carbon quantum dots co-decorated TiO2 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalytic H2 production and reaction mechanism

coredata.dc:description: TiO2-based composites are promising photocatalysts for efficient generation of the clean energy, H2, from water splitting. Herein, series of Pd nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-decorated TiO2 nanosheets (NSs), Pd-CQDs/TiO2, were designed by overall considering taking advantages of the three components: highly active TiO2 NSs, highly conductive CQDs and electron sink of Pd. Series of photocatalytic experiments were performed and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was explored. The Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 exhibited an excellent photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate (HER), which is 901.5, 41.8 and 1.7 times higher than those of the bare TiO2, 0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd/TiO2, respectively. The synergistic effect of the CQDs and Pd was found responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2. Besides, the CQDs was found facilitating complete conversion of Pd2+ to metallic Pd on the surface of TiO2 NSs, which may offer a new thought for design of other composite materials.

head.ce:title: Pd and carbon quantum dots co-decorated TiO2 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalytic H2 production and reaction mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin, 300384, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin, 300384, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2-based composites are promising photocatalysts for efficient generation of the clean energy, H2, from water splitting. Herein, series of Pd nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-decorated TiO2 nanosheets (NSs), Pd-CQDs/TiO2, were designed by overall considering taking advantages of the three components: highly active TiO2 NSs, highly conductive CQDs and electron sink of Pd. Series of photocatalytic experiments were performed and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was explored. The Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 exhibited an excellent photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate (HER), which is 901.5, 41.8 and 1.7 times higher than those of the bare TiO2, 0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd/TiO2, respectively. The synergistic effect of the CQDs and Pd was found responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2. Besides, the CQDs was found facilitating complete conversion of Pd2+ to metallic Pd on the surface of TiO2 NSs, which may offer a new thought for design of other composite materials.

ce:section.ce:para: As a clean and renewable energy source, H2 has received much attention from the researchers. The great idea on H2 production by solar-driven water splitting over semiconductors has been introduced by A. Fujushima and his team members [1]. Titanium dioxides (TiO2), as a promising semiconductor for photocatalytic H2 production, have the advantages in highly active, cheap, non-toxic and stable etc. [2–4]. However, the broad forbidden band of the anatase TiO2 nanomaterials (∼3.2 eV) [5,6] and bad charge carrier separation would generally result in poor visible light utilization and low photocatalytic activity [7,8]. Therefore, modifying TiO2 nanomaterials with co-catalysts, as one of the good strategies, is necessary in order to significantly improve the photocatalytic H2 production activity [9].

The reported co-catalysts for decorations of the TiO2 nanomaterials include noble and non-noble metals [10–14], non-metals [15–18], semiconductors with narrow band gaps [19,20], dye photosensitizers [21] and up-conversion fluorescers [22,23]. As an important non-metal cocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention owing to their characteristics, such as low toxicity, high chemical stability, wide spectral absorption and fast electron transfer [24–31]. So far, some CQDs/TiO2 composites have been reported, where the CQDs served as photosensitizers, electron mediators or spectral converter [32–34]. For instance, Yu et al. prepared a CQDs/P25 composite with an enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity [34]. Kang et al. designed a CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst for photo-degradation of methylene blue and found that the hybridization of CQDs with TiO2 extended the light response of the TiO2 into the visible region [32]. Pan et al. reported a CQDs/TiO2 nanotube composite for photo-degradation of rhodamine B dye [33]. Although loading a single co-catalyst CQDs on TiO2 can enhance its photocatalytic activity to certain extent, there is still a large space remained for further improvement by loading dual co-catalysts with different properties.

In this work, for the first time the ternary composites Pd-CQDs/TiO2 were designed, prepared and characterized. In order to optimize the photocatalytic HER and reveal the photocatalytic mechanism, series of photocatalytic H2 production experiments were performed under various conditions. The Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst displayed high photocatalytic HER and cycling stability.

Following the previous works [40,41], the TiO2 nanomaterials were prepared by a hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 24 h, using 5 mL of Ti(OBu)4 and 0.8 mL of HF as the raw materials. The as-obtained white precipitate underwent centrifugation, washing with deionized water and ethanol, drying at 60 °C in an oven and finally it was ground into powders for further use. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) dispersion was prepared by a hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 24 h [25], where 10 g of poplar leaves and 38 mL of high purity water were used. The concentration of the CQDs dispersion was measured by an evaporation-weighing method, which is 2.1 mol L−1. The CQDs/TiO2 binary composites with various CQD quantity were prepared by a hydrothermal method at 120 °C for 4 h, using 0.2 g of the TiO2 powder, 10 mL of high purity water and various volumes (0.2, 0.5 or 0.8 mL) of the CQDs dispersion. The resulting powder samples were named as 0.2CQDs/TiO2, 0.5CQDs/TiO2 and 0.8CQDs/TiO2, respectively.

The series of Pd-CQDs/TiO2 samples were prepared by photodeposition method. Firstly, 100 mg of the 0.5CQDs/TiO2 powder was well dispersed in 40 mL of high purity water by sonicating for 12 min. Secondly, certain volume (10, 50, 100 or 150 μL) of the PdCl2 solution (10 g L−1) was put into the suspension above under constant stirring. Finally the mixture underwent photodeposition reactions for 30 min irradiated by an Xe lamp. The as-obtained ternary composite samples were specified as 0.1%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2, 0.5%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2, 1.0%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 and 1.5%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2, respectively. When the pure TiO2 and 100 μL of PdCl2 solution were applied for the photodeposition, the resulting product was called as Pd/TiO2. If 100 μL of PdCl2 solution and the xCQDs/TiO2 (x = 0.2, 0.5 or 0.8) were used for photodeposition, the products were named as Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2, Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2, respectively. According to the concentration of the CQDs dispersion, the weight percentages of the input CQDs in the composites were estimated, which are 2.4 %, 5.8 % and 9.9 %, respectively. However, based on the results measured by a combustion method, the real contents of the CQDs in the samples Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2, Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2 are 0.73 %, 0.83 % and 1.20 %, respectively.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer, Japan) patterns were measured to determine the crystal structure of the samples. The SEM, mapping images and EDS spectra were performed on a scanning electron microscopy (ZEISS MERLIN Compact, Germany) and Energy Disperse Spectroscopy analyzer. The TEM and HRTEM images were taken to observe the microstructure of the TiO2 composite on a transmission electron microscopy (Talos F200X, FEI, USA). The UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were recorded on a solid UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600, Japan), using BaSO4 as the reference material. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS) were carried out on an Esca Lab 250Xi system (Thermo Scientific). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples were performed on a a surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, TriStar II, America). Fluorescence spectra were measured by a steady-state fluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS1000, England).

Referring to our previous work [41], the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and transient photocurrent responses were measured with a three-electrode system (counter electrode: Pt mesh; reference electrode: Ag/AgCl) to study the charge transfer rate and separation of the TiO2-based samples, where an on-line electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, China) was used and the working electrodes were coated by the as-prepared samples. Similarly, the Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots were also recorded on the electrochemical workstation, where 1 × 1 cm2 of FTO glass electrode coated by the sample was used as the working electrode and the applied frequency was 1000 Hz.

The photocatalytic H2 production activities of the series of TiO2-based samples were tested on an online evaluation system, (CEAULIGHT, CEL-SPH2N, China) referring to our previous work [41]. An Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300) with controllable light power density was used as the UV–visible light source. Here, less mass of the TiO2-based catalyst (10 mg) was put into the reaction vessel that contained 100 mL of sacrificial reagent-water mixed solution with volume ratio of 1:9, which underwent constant stirring for 30 min in dark before the photocatalytic reactions. The applied sacrificial reagents included lactic acid, triethanolamine and methanol. Generally, the light power density was adjusted to 340 mW cm−2. The photocatalytic reaction temperature was kept constant during reactions by using a cooling circulation system. The product H2 was measured using an on-line gas chromatograph (GC-7900, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure, morphology and composition of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structures of the pure TiO2 nanomaterial and its composites were investigated by the XRD patterns. As seen in Fig. 1 , all the diffraction peaks of the samples correspond to a tetragonal phase of anatase TiO2. The indexed card is JCPDS 21–1272 and the cell parameters are a = 3.785 Å, b = 3.785 Å and c = 9.514 Å. With loading the Pd nanoparticles and/or CQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanomaterial, no extra diffraction peaks for CQDs and/or Pd were present, probably owing to their poor crystallinity or low contents. Compared to the pure TiO2, the diffraction peaks for the composite TiO2 samples were hardly changed except slight increase in peak intensity, indicating that the loading of CQDs and/or Pd did not influence the crystal structure of the TiO2 nanomaterial. The slight increase in the peak intensity after loading of Pd and/or CQDs suggests slight increase in crystallinity, which could be in favor of the photocatalytic reactions.

The average crystal sizes (D) of the as-prepared TiO2 nanomaterial and its composites can be estimated by using the Debye-Scherrer formula: D = 0.89λ/(β cosθ) [42], based on the strong diffraction peak at the low angle (101). It was found that the D values for the binary and ternary TiO2 composites increased slightly (13.7–14.6 nm), compared to that of the pure TiO2 nanomaterial (13.1 nm), which may be due to the secondary growth of the TiO2 primary crystals upon heating and/or illumination during loading of CQDs and/or Pd. The relevant parameters (FWHM, 2θ and D values) were tabulated in Table S1 of the electronic supporting information (ESI).

The SEM images of the pure TiO2, 0.5CQDs/TiO2, Pd/TiO2 and Pd-CQDs/TiO2 composites (Fig. 2 and Fig. S1 in the ESI) exhibit the similar nanosheet-like morphology. Neither the Pd nanoparticles nor the CQDs loading changed the morphology of the TiO2 NSs. The side length and thickness of the TiO2 NSs are about tens of nanometers and several nanometers, respectively. The sizes of the nanosheets for the samples Pd/TiO2 and Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 composites are slightly smaller than those of the pure TiO2 and 0.5CQDs/TiO2, which may be caused by the photodeposition process in the PdCl2 solution.

To confirm the successful loading of Pd and CQDs on the TiO2 NSs, the element mapping images were taken, where the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample was chosen as a representative. Fig. 3 a–e displays uniform distribution of the elements Ti, O, Pd, F and C in the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 composite, confirming the successful loading of Pd and C. The sparsely dispersed dots for the Pd element (Fig. 3c) suggests its low content, which was confirmed by the EDS result (Fig. 3f), as low as 0.14 % atomic ratio of Pd. For the Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2 and Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2 samples (Fig. S2 in the ESI), the Pd contents are similar, 0.14 % and 0.16 %, respectively. Small amount of F element was present due to the residual of raw material HF. The high atomic ratio of C element could be attributed to adventitious carbon (main) and the carbon from the CQDs (minor).

In order to clearly observe the microstructure of the ternary composite, the TEM and HRTEM images of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 were taken. As seen in Fig. 4 a, the TiO2 displays rectangular nanosheet morphology with the thickness of ∼5 nm and side length of ∼40 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 4b) confirms the successful loading of Pd nanoparticles (5–10 nm in diameter) and CQDs (3 nm in diameter) on the surface of TiO2 NSs. The lattice spacing of 0.22 nm belongs to the (111) facet of a metallic Pd and the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm corresponds to the (101) facet of an anatase TiO2. The lattice fringe for CQD is not so clear, confirming its poor crystallinity. In order to observe the CQDs clearly, the TEM image of the CQDs dispersion was taken (Fig. S3 in the ESI). The particle sizes of the CQDs are about 3–5 nm.

XPS spectra were measured to confirm the surface composition and chemical states of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample. To reveal the important role of CQDs in the ternary composite, XPS spectra of the binary composite Pd/TiO2 were also measured. As seen in the survey XPS spectra (Fig. 5 a), the two samples both possess strong peaks for Ti 2p and O 1s, and weak peaks for C 1s, Pd 3d and F 1s, confirming that the two samples are composed of TiO2 mainly with small amount of Pd, F and C. The relative higher C1s peak for the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 could be caused by the loading of CQDs.

The core level of XPS spectra for O 1s (Fig. 5b) display that the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample includes three peaks after Gaussian fitting, locating at 529.8, 531.7 and 533.3 eV, which correspond to the oxygen in Ti–O bonds, surface adsorption oxygen and oxygen in the CO bonds [43], respectively, suggesting the successful loading of CQDs on the TiO2 NSs. The Ti 2p spectrum for the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (Fig. 5c) shows that the peaks at the binding energies of 458.6 and 464.3 eV belong to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of Ti4+, respectively. One notices that the binding energies for O1s and Ti 2p of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample were slightly lower than those of the Pd/TiO2, which could be attributed to the loading of CQDs. A small amount of Ti4+ could capture some electrons, forming Ti3+ because the electronegativity of C element is lower than O. The slight drop in oxidation number of the part of Ti4+ resulted in slight decrease in binding energy of Ti 2p. The formation of small amount of Ti3+ could be helpful for enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

The Pd 3d XPS spectrum for the Pd/TiO2 (Fig. 5d) includes four peaks after Gaussian fitting. The peaks locating at the binding energies of 335.0 and 340.2 eV correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Pd0, while the peaks at 336.6 and 342.0 eV belong to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Pd2+, respectively [41,44]. For the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample, however, only two peaks with the binding energies of 334.9 and 340.4 eV were observed, which were attributed to Pd0. The absence of Pd2+ peaks for the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample indicates that Pd2+ was completely reduced to metal Pd in the presence of CQDs in the photodeposition process, which could be in favor of the photocatalytic reactions.

The XPS spectra for C 1s of the two samples (Fig. 5e) both include three peaks, locating at 284.8 eV, 286.4/286.5 eV and 288.7/288.8 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC (from reference material), C–O and CO bond (from CQDs and/or residual of raw material butyl titanate), respectively [45]. The relatively higher peak at 288.7 eV for the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample further confirms the successful loading of CQDs on the TiO2 NSs because the C atom from the CQDs may form CO bonds with O atom from the TiO2 nanosheets [33]. The F 1s peaks at 684 eV (Fig. 5f) correspond to F−, physically adsorbed on the TiO2 NSs [41,46].

The light absorption capacity and band gap energies of the TiO2-based samples were investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS). As seen in Fig. 6 a, the synergistic effect of the metallic Pd and nonmetal CQDs significantly intensified the visible light absorption of the ternary composite Pd-CQDs/TiO2. Particularly, the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 displayed the strongest absorption on visible light, which may excite more electrons for photocatalytic H+ reduction reactions, beneficial to enhancement of the photocatalytic H2 production activity.

The absorption band edges of the ternary composites red shifted, resulting in the narrowed band gaps, as seen in Fig. 6b. The band gap energies (E
g ) of the TiO2-based catalysts were calculated by the formula (αhν) 1/n = A (hν - E
g ), where n = 2 was used for calculation [47]. It was found that the band gap energies of the samples (Table S1 in the ESI) decreased distinctly from 3.04 eV for the pure TiO2 to 2.39 eV for the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2, implying that the co-loading of Pd and CQDs may form two surface states in the forbidden band of the TiO2 NSs. The surface state formed by the metallic Pd nanoparticles should be below the conduction band of the TiO2 NSs and the other one formed by the CQDs could be above the valence band of the TiO2 NSs. This indicates that the synergistic effect of the Pd and CQDs caused the narrowed band gaps of the ternary composites Pd-CQDs/TiO2, which could lead to more photo-induced electrons and holes participating in the photocatalytic redox reactions, and consequently enhance the photocatalytic H2 production activity.

Fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured to get insight on separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes on the surface of the TiO2-based catalysts. Fig. 6c demonstrates that the loading of the small amount of Pd nanoparticles or/and CQDs decreased distinctly the intensities of fluorescence emission peaks. Particularly, the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 sample possesses the lowest peak intensity, suggesting the minimum recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes. Thus, the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 displays the best charge separation.

The EIS spectra and transient photocurrent curves of the working electrodes made by the series of TiO2-based samples were performed to explore the charge transfer efficiency. The smaller radius of arc in the EIS spectra corresponds to the lower charge transfer resistance. Fig. 6d demonstrates that the measured resistance values follow the order of TiO2 > Pd/TiO2 > 0.5CQDs/TiO2 > Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2 > Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2 > Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2, indicating that the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 composite catalyst has the lowest charge transfer resistance, implying the highest electron transfer rate. The transient photocurrent densities of the samples (Fig. 6e) are in the sequence of Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 > Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2 > Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2 ≈ Pd/TiO2 > 0.5CQDs/TiO2 > TiO2. The higher photocurrent density corresponds to the higher carrier migration efficiency. The results in Fig. 6d and e indicate that the synergistic effect of the Pd nanoparticles and appropriate amount of CQDs significantly accelerated the charge transfer of the ternary composites. Particularly, the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst has the highest charge transfer efficiency, which may result in an enhanced photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 production activities and reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To maximize the photocatalytic HER of the ternary composite Pd-CQDs/TiO2 and explore the photocatalytic reaction mechanism, series of control experiments were performed. Firstly, the effect of Pd content on the photocatalytic H2 production activity was investigated in a methanol-water mixture at 25 °C with the light power density of 340 mW cm−2. As seen in Fig. 7 a, the photocatalytic activities of the x%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (x = 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 5.0) catalysts change with increasing the Pd contents. The results show that the H2 productions increase from 8.5 mmol g−1 cat to 66.4 mmol g−1 cat with increasing the Pd contents from 0.1 % to 1.0 % (input quantity). Further increasing the Pd content to 1.5 %, the H2 production was distinctly reduced to 22.4 mmol g−1 cat, which may be caused by the surface shielding effect of the excess Pd nanoparticles. When the input Pd content was increased to 5.0 %, the H2 production was similar to that of the 1.5%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2. Fig. 7b displays that the photocatalytic HER of the 1.0%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (9.5 mmol h−1 g−1 cat) is much higher than those of the 0.1%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (1.2 mmol h−1 g−1 cat), 0.5%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (5.8 mmol h−1 g−1 cat), 1.5%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (3.2 mmol h−1 g−1 cat) and 5.0%Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (3.1 mmol h−1 g−1 cat). Thus, 1.0 % Pd was found an optimal Pd content.

Reaction temperature is one of the vital factors to the photocatalytic activity of a catalyst. Hence, we studied the photocatalytic H2 production performances of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst at different temperatures with methanol as a sacrificial reagent. As demonstrated in Fig. 7c and d, the H2 productions increase nearly linearly with time at the temperature of 6 °C, 15 °C and 25 °C. Much more H2 (66.4 mmol g−1 cat) was generated within 7 h reactions at 25 °C than at the lower temperature of 6 °C (29.2 mmol g−1 cat). The photocatalytic HER of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 at 25 °C (9.5 mmol h−1 g−1 cat) is 2.3 times higher than that of 6 °C (4.2 mmol h−1 g−1 cat), indicating that the higher temperature is in favor of the photocatalytic H2 production. The higher reaction temperature, such as ≥30 °C, would cause evaporation of water. Therefore, the room temperature, 25 °C, was used for the following tests.

The effect of sacrificial reagent on the photocatalytic activity was studied, where 10 % volume percentage of methanol, lactic acid or triethanolamine was used as the sacrificial reagent (light power density of 340 mW cm−2). Fig. 7e demonstrates that the produced H2 gases increase nearly linearly with time in both lactic acid-water and methanol-water mixtures. The H2 production in the lactic acid-water system is the highest, reaching 82.1 mmol g−1 cat after 7 h reactions. The corresponding photocatalytic HER is 11.7 mmol h−1 g−1 cat, higher than those in methanol and triethanolamine (Fig. 7f). This may be due to the fact that lactic acid can release more H+ to the reaction system owing to its acid characteristics, and consequently more H2 was produced. In the triethanolamine-water system, the hydrogen production increased very slowly after 5 h reactions and the average HER was only 4.5 mmol h−1 g−1 cat. The results above indicate that lactic acid is a good sacrificial reagent, which will be used for the following tests.

The effect of incident light intensity on the photocatalytic H2 production activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 was investigated by adjusting the light power densities, 60, 100, 200, 340 and 540 mW cm−2. As seen in Fig. 7g and h, the photocatalytic HERs (2.1, 3.0, 3.5, 11.7 and 13.4 mmol h−1 g−1 cat, respectively) increased gradually with increasing the light power densities. The higher light intensity means more energy available to excite more electrons for the photocatalytic H+ reduction reactions, corresponding to the higher H2 production. However, the higher light intensity implies more energy consumption. In order to save energy, the light power density of 340 mW cm−2 was applied in the following tests.

The effect of CQDs content on the photocatalytic H2 production activity of the Pd-CQDs/TiO2 was investigated to further optimize the HER. As seen in Fig. 8 a and b, the as-prepared samples Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2, Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2 displayed enhanced activities to different extent, compared to the bare TiO2 and the binary composites, 0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd/TiO2. Noticeably, the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 exhibited the highest HER of 11.7 mmol h−1 g−1 cat within 7 h reactions, which is 901.5, 41.8 and 1.7 times higher than those of the bare TiO2 (0.01 mmol h−1 g−1 cat), 0.5CQDs/TiO2 (0.3 mmol h−1 g−1 cat) and Pd/TiO2 (7.1 mmol h−1 g−1 cat), respectively. The H2 production of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 is much higher than the sum total of the 0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd/TiO2, indicating clearly there is synergistic effect of the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2. Besides, as proved by the XPS result, the presence of CQDs facilitated complete conversion of Pd2+ to metallic Pd in the photodeposition process, leading to the better charge carrier separation, and ultimately enhancement of the photocatalytic H2 production over the ternary composites Pd-CQDs/TiO2. The highest photocatalytic HER of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 could be attributed to the fact that the synergistic effect of the optimal amount of CQDs and Pd nanoparticles not only significantly intensified light absorption, narrowed the band gap, but also effectively improved the photogenerated electron-hole transfer and separation. Excess CQDs on the TiO2 surface may shield part of active sites or become the recombination center of electrons-holes, leading to drop in the photocatalytic activity.

The BET surface areas of the samples were measured to check if they are the key factor to the photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. S4 of the ESI, the BET surface areas of the binary composites Pd/TiO2 (96.9 m2 g−1) and 0.5CQDs/TiO2 (96.3 m2 g−1) are slightly lower than that of the pure TiO2 (98.8 m2 g−1). With increasing the quantity of CQD dispersion, the BET surface areas of the ternary composites Pd-0.2CQDs/TiO2 (93.4 m2 g−1), Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 (88.1 m2 g−1) and Pd-0.8CQDs/TiO2 (82.3 m2 g−1) gradually decreased. Normally, the higher specific surface area of a catalyst is beneficial to promoting the photocatalytic activity. These results indicate that the slight decrease in the BET surface areas of the TiO2 composites did not reduce their activities. Thus, for the present work, the BET surface area may not be the key factor to the photocatalytic activity.

Compared to the related works in literature [48–53] (Table 1 ), the photocatalytic HERs in the methanol or lactic acid aqueous solutions over the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 are much higher, but they are lower than that of the 1.6%Pt-doped TiO2 nanotubes (19.2 mmol h−1g−1) [54]. Generally, the Pt cocatalyst may increase the activity of TiO2 maximally due to its high work function. However, Pt is much more expensive than Pd, which is not conducive to practical applications. The results in Table 1 display that the catalyst, light source and sacrificial reagent greatly influence the photocatalytic H2 production activity.

The photocatalytic cycling stability of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 ternary composite catalyst was investigated. As seen in Fig. 8c and d, the catalyst displayed good cycling stability. After three cycles (21 h in total) of the photocatalytic H2 production reactions, the photocatalytic HER of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 only reduced by 11.5 %. The high cycling stability of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 composite catalyst could be attributed to the high structural stability of the TiO2 NSs, as shown in Fig. S5 of the ESI. The crystal phase and morphology of the used Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst hardly changed after three cycles of the photocatalytic H2 production reactions. However, the diffraction peak intensity for the used catalyst decreased to certain extent, indicating the lower crystallinity that could be unfavorable to the photocatalytic reactions. In addition, the XPS results (Fig. S6 in the ESI) for the used and fresh catalysts display that the Pd 3d peaks became weak and the Pd2+ peak appeared for the used catalyst, suggesting that the part of metallic Pd fell off from the composite catalyst and the part of Pd0 was oxidized to Pd2+. Meanwhile the C–O and CO peaks became relatively stronger, indicating that some lactic acid molecules may adsorb on the catalyst surface. These changes in the surface structures could be the main reason for the decreased HER after three cycles of reactions.

The kinetic curve of H2 evolution over the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst was measured in the lactic acid-water mixture, where 32 h of continuous photocatalytic H2 evolution reactions were performed without degas. As seen in Fig. 8e, the H2 production increases nearly linearly in the first 26 h. After that, less and less H2 was generated with prolonging the reaction time, indicating that the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction was approaching the equilibrium or the photocatalyst was becoming devitalized. The long period of photocatalytic H2 evolution is great meaningful to practical applications.

Based on the experimental results above, the photocatalytic H2 production mechanism over the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst was proposed and displayed in Scheme 1 a. Under UV–vis light illumination, the photogenerated electrons in the valence band (VB) of the TiO2 NSs are excited to its CB by capturing energy from the incident UV–vis light. The photogenerated electrons in the CB of the TiO2 NSs will further transfer to the electron sink, Pd nanoparticles [11,17,56–58], and CQDs (Φ = 4.99 eV). The photogenerated holes in the VB of the TiO2 NSs tend to transfer to the VB of the CQDs under the driving force of potential difference. These migrations of the photogenerated electrons and holes would greatly promote charge separation and depress their recombination, which is one of the key factors to enhancement of the photocatalytic H2 production activity.

It was worthy to mention that the presence of CQDs facilitated complete conversion of Pd2+ to metallic Pd on the TiO2 surface. H+ can capture the photogenerated electrons from the CB of the TiO2 NSs, forming H2, because the CB potential of the TiO2 NSs (−0.32 eV) is more negative than the reduction potential of H+/H2 (0 eV). Besides, the small size of Pd co-catalyst can provide more highly active sites for H+ reduction [58–60]. The favorable hydrogen adsorption free energy on the Pd nanoparticles [60] enables more H+ adsorbed on the surface of Pd, where the as-migrated photogenerated electrons reduce H+ to H2. As a result, the photocatalytic H2 production activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst was enhanced dramatically. In addition, H+ can also obtain photogenerated electrons from CB of the CQDs for reduction reactions due to its more negative CB potential (−0.21 eV), compared to the reduction potential of H+/H2. Thus, the photocatalytic H2 production activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst was further increased. The sacrificial reagent (lactic acid) and water molecules are oxidized by the photogenerated holes in the VBs of the TiO2 NSs and CQDs, forming oxidant products (Ox), accompanying with releasing a large number of H+ ions. In this process, the sacrificial reagent not only contributed H+ to the reaction system but also consumed holes that reduced recombination of photogenerated electrons/holes, and thereby enhanced the photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic behavior of the Pd modified carbon quantum dots (without TiO2) has been studied in the same reaction conditions. The result shows that no H2 peak was detected after 3 h photocatalytic reactions, suggesting that the photogenerated electrons for the photocatalytic H+ reductions were generated on the main component, the TiO2 NSs, although in principle small amount of photogenerated electrons should also be generated on the CQDs. The tiny amount of photogenerated electrons on the CQDs were not able to produce detectable H2. This result further confirmed the synergistic effect of Pd and CQDs on the TiO2 NSs. The photocatalytic performance of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst under visible light irradiation (>420 nm) has also been explored. The result displays that no apparent H2 peak was detected after 3 h photocatalytic reactions, indicating that the small amount of CQD is not able to trigger obvious visible light catalytic H2 evolution reactions although in principle the photogenerated electrons are available in the CB of the CQDs for H+ reduction. The quantity of CQDs is too small and the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CQDs are easier to recombine with the holes in the VB due to the narrow band gap. The electron transfer from the CB of CQDs to the CB of TiO2 NSs is not feasible because the more positive CB potential of the CQDs, which makes the CQDs failure as a photosensitizer. Thus, the CQDs mainly function as the electron reservoirs to trap the photogenerated electrons from the CB of TiO2 NSs, which promoted the separation of electrons and holes on the TiO2 surface, and ultimately enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst.

The synergistic effect of the highly conductive CQDs and electron-sink nature of the Pd nanoparticles not only optimized the band structure (Scheme 1b, plotted based on the measured VB potentials, E
VB
, and CB potentials, E
CB ), intensified visible light absorption, but also provided the electron/hole capture centers that significantly accelerated electron/hole transfer and separation, and consequently improved the utilization of photogenerated electrons and holes, resulting in much enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 composite.

The Pd-CQDs/TiO2 ternary composite catalysts with various contents of Pd and CQDs were prepared successfully. The co-loading of the nonmetal CQDs and noble metal Pd not only intensified visible light absorption of the TiO2 composites, narrowed the band gaps by introducing two surface states in the forbidden band, but also improved the charge transfer rate and separation. Series of photocatalytic H2 production tests show that the sacrificial reagent, reaction temperature, light intensity, contents of Pd and CQDs all influence the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. The Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 catalyst exhibited the highest photocatalytic HER, 13.4 mmol h−1 g−1 cat, in the lactic acid-water mixture at 25 °C under UV–vis light irradiation with light power density of 540 mW cm−2. The HER of the Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2 was 901.5, 41.8 and 1.7 times higher than those of the bare TiO2, 0.5CQDs/TiO2 and Pd/TiO2, respectively. The photocatalytic reaction mechanism was proposed based on experimental results. It was found that the synergistic effects of the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs on light excitation and absorption performance, energy band structure of the catalyst and charge transfer rate/separation significantly improved the photocatalytic H2 production activity of the ternary composite Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2. In addition, it was found that the presence of CQDs facilitated complete conversion of Pd2+ to metallic Pd on the surface of TiO2 NSs, which provided more highly active sites for H+ reduction, promoted the charge transfer/separation, and ultimately enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the ternary composite Pd-0.5CQDs/TiO2. This work provides a new thought for synthesis of composite catalysts with the help of CQDs.
